title: Emergency Guide: Puppy Swallowed Hearing Aid Battery
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a hearing aid battery, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is required to prevent tissue burns.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingesting a hearing aid battery is a life-threatening emergency that can cause severe internal burns within 15 minutes; seek immediate veterinary care and do not wait for symptoms.

What are hearing aid batteries and why are they dangerous for my dog?

  • Small, circular power sources (also known as button or disc batteries) that pose a severe threat to puppies and dogs.
  • Unlike standard batteries, they cause rapid internal damage by leaking corrosive alkaline material.
  • They generate an electrical current that creates caustic soda inside the body.

Is it an emergency if my dog swallows a hearing aid battery?

  • Urgency level: High. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate veterinary intervention.
  • Do not wait for symptoms; deep, irreversible burns to the esophagus, stomach, or intestinal lining can occur within 15 to 30 minutes.
  • These injuries can lead to tissue perforation and severe internal infection.

How can taking a photo of the battery packaging help my veterinarian?

  • Take a clear photo of the original packaging or an identical spare battery immediately to assist the triage team.
  • Allows identification of the specific size, voltage, and chemical composition (such as lithium or alkaline).
  • Helps the vet determine the level of corrosive risk and decide if surgical removal or endoscopic retrieval is necessary.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of alkaline batteries, commonly found in household electronics like hearing aids, poses a risk of gastrointestinal tract corrosive injury and foreign body obstruction in puppies. Alkaline gel within the batteries causes liquefactive necrosis on contact, leading to burns. Lithium "button" batteries can lodge in the esophagus and generate a current against the esophageal walls, resulting in circular ulcers. If the battery was swallowed intact without chewing, emesis may be induced unless the casing is suspected to be punctured. Radiographs are helpful to confirm the diagnosis and location of the battery, although some disk batteries may not be visible. For ingested disk batteries, administer 20 mL boluses of tap water every 15 minutes to decrease the severity and delay esophageal ulceration. Batteries that do not pass through the pylorus within 48 hours may require surgical or endoscopic removal. Surgical removal is indicated if the battery is punctured to prevent gastric or intestinal ulceration.

Chapter: Toxicology, Gastroenterology, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3003)

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of battery ingestion?
faqs.answer: Symptoms may include heavy drooling, refusal to eat, vomiting, oral ulcers, or a blackened appearance on the tongue. However, many dogs show no outward signs until severe internal damage has already occurred.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting if a battery is swallowed. The corrosive material can cause more damage to the esophagus on the way back up, or the battery could become lodged in the throat, causing a blockage.

faqs.question: How do vets treat battery ingestion?
faqs.answer: Vets will typically use X-rays to locate the battery. Depending on its location and type, they may use an endoscope to retrieve it or perform emergency surgery if it has already caused a perforation.
